outbound_official.note: No official Belgium government outbound visa-list page located. Belgium's FPS Foreign Affairs (diplomatie.belgium.be) publishes per-country travel advice pages for Belgian citizens (e.g. diplomatie.belgium.be/fr/pays/[country]) but maintains no single consolidated list of visa-free, visa-on-arrival, e-visa, or ETA destinations for Belgian ordinary passport holders comparable to India's MEA VFFIN page. Rely on destination-side data.

name: Belgium

visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens have freedom of movement in Belgium and the Schengen area; no visa required for any duration. They may enter with a valid national identity card or passport.

eta.nationality: All Annex II (Regulation (EU) 2018/1806) visa-exempt third-country nationals
eta.notes: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) will be required for all visa-exempt third-country nationals travelling to Schengen states including Belgium. ETIAS is expected to become operational in Q4 2026, with mandatory enforcement from April 2027. It costs EUR 7, is valid for 3 years or until passport expiry (whichever is earlier), and permits stays of up to 90 days within any 180-day period. EU/EEA and Swiss nationals are exempt from ETIAS. Not yet operational as of June 2026.

visa_policy.notes: Belgium is part of the Schengen Area. Visa policy for short stays (up to 90 days in any 180-day period) is governed by EU Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 (consolidated version 03.02.2025). All EU/EEA citizens have freedom of movement with no stay limit. Vanuatu was permanently moved to Annex I (visa required) by Regulation (EU) 2025/11, effective 14 January 2025. Kosovo became visa-free from 1 January 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2023/850. ETIAS authorization will be required for all visa-exempt nationals once launched (expected Q4 2026, mandatory from April 2027). Belgium grants visa exemption only to persons travelling on refugee passports (travel documents) issued by the United States of America, on the condition that they have legal long-term residence in the US; refugee travel documents from Canada, Australia, UK, or other countries do NOT qualify for Belgian visa exemption.

conditional_access.conditions: Third-country nationals holding a valid long-stay visa (national long-stay visa, more than 90 days) issued by any Schengen Member State may travel visa-free to Belgium for up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Five identity parameters must match 100% between travel document and visa: name, first name, sex, date of birth, nationality. For stays exceeding 90 days in Belgium specifically, a Belgian residence permit or Belgian D visa is always required.

Third-country nationals holding a valid residence permit issued by any Schengen Member State may travel visa-free to Belgium for up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Five identity parameters must match 100% between travel document and permit: name, first name, sex, date of birth, nationality. Certain French temporary receipts (récépissé) and German Aufenthaltstitel without specific endorsements lack visa-free value. Cyprus and Ireland residence permits in principle have no Schengen visa-free value except those explicitly issued to family members of EU citizens under Directive 2004/38. UK residence cards and visas carry no Schengen visa-free value.

credential.subtype: residence permit issued to EU citizen family member under Directive 2004/38

conditional_access.conditions: Irish residence permits are generally excluded from Schengen visa-free travel. Exception: permits that explicitly state they were issued to a family member of an EU citizen or issued pursuant to Directive 2004/38 may grant visa-free access. This exception covers residence permits only — never visas. Each document must be individually verified against the official IBZ list.

credential.subtype: residence permit issued to EU citizen family member under Directive 2004/38

conditional_access.conditions: Cypriot residence permits are generally excluded from Schengen visa-free travel. Exception: permits that explicitly state they were issued to a family member of an EU citizen or issued pursuant to Directive 2004/38 may grant visa-free access. This exception covers residence permits only — never visas. Each document must be individually verified against the official IBZ list.

Belgium grants visa exemption only to persons travelling on refugee passports (travel documents) issued by the United States of America, on the condition that they have legal long-term residence in the US. Refugee or stateless person travel documents issued by Canada, Australia, UK, or other countries do NOT qualify for Belgian visa exemption. Source: EU Commission national derogations document (section 2.2.1 — BNL column) and IBZ exceptions page.

cbi.notes: Belgium does not operate a dedicated Citizenship-by-Investment program. No official Belgian government source lists a CBI scheme. Naturalization is possible after 5 years of lawful residence (including via investor/entrepreneur routes), subject to language proficiency in French, Dutch, or German and demonstrated social and economic integration. The Belgian Immigration Office (IBZ) and Federal Public Service Foreign Affairs websites confirm no direct CBI pathway.

programs.name: Professional Card for Self-Employed / Entrepreneur
programs.notes: Third-country nationals wishing to conduct self-employed activities in Belgium for more than 90 days must obtain a Professional Card from the competent regional authority (Flanders, Wallonia, Brussels-Capital, or German-speaking Community). There is no official fixed minimum investment amount. After 5 years of lawful residence, applicants may apply for long-term resident status. Application for the D visa is made at the Belgian embassy/consulate, supported by the regional Professional Card decision.

programs.name: Single Permit (Salaried Employee)
programs.notes: Combined work and residence permit for non-EU nationals employed more than 90 days in Belgium (since 3 January 2019). Employer applies on behalf of the worker to the relevant regional authority. Region makes work authorization decision; Immigration Office decides on residence. Standard decision period: 4 months from admissibility (extendable). After 5 years of lawful and uninterrupted residence, applicants may obtain long-term resident status (L card). EU Blue Card holders benefit from combined EU residency calculation for long-term resident purposes.

programs.name: Long-Term Resident Status (EC Long-Term Residence Permit)
programs.notes: Requires 5 years of legal and uninterrupted residence in Belgium. Financial condition: stable, regular and sufficient means of subsistence (minimum EUR 1,038/month for single person, plus EUR 346 per dependent). Medical insurance required. Must not pose danger to public order. Study and vocational training periods count at 50%. EU Blue Card holders may combine EU-wide residency (minimum 2 years immediately before application in Belgium). Grants L card (EU long-term residence permit). Processing: Immigration Office has 5 months to decide.

programs.name: EU Blue Card (Highly Qualified Worker)
programs.eligibility: Third-country nationals with higher education qualifications or high professional skills, employed by a Belgian employer under a Single Permit application. Identified with code B29 on the visa/permit.
programs.processing_time: 4 months from admissibility (extendable); region makes work authorization decision while Immigration Office decides residence
programs.notes: EU Blue Card holders benefit from family reunification without proof of sufficient means of subsistence. Can combine EU-wide residency across Schengen states for long-term resident status (minimum 2 years in Belgium immediately before application). Applied via Single Permit procedure by employer.

programs.name: Researcher Permit
programs.eligibility: Third-country nationals conducting scientific research in Belgium under a hosting agreement with a recognized research institution.
programs.notes: Listed as a separate category on the IBZ work themes page. Requires hosting agreement with recognized research organization. Long-term mobility option also available for researchers already authorized in another EU member state.

programs.name: Intra-Corporate Transfer (ICT) Permit
programs.eligibility: Managers, specialists, or trainee employees being transferred within a multinational enterprise to a Belgian entity for more than 90 days.
programs.notes: Listed as 'Worker ICT' category on the IBZ work themes page. Separate procedure from the standard Single Permit.

programs.name: Orientation Year After Higher Studies
programs.eligibility: Third-country national graduates of Belgian higher education institutions who wish to seek employment or start a business after completing their studies.
programs.notes: Allows graduates a period to look for work or start self-employed activity in Belgium before converting to a work or entrepreneur permit.

agreements: Benelux Convention on short-stay visas (11 April 1960, Article 3) — harmonised Benelux visa policy for diplomatic passport exemptions
EU-Cape Verde Visa Facilitation Agreement (OJ L 282, 24.10.2013)
EU Regulation 2023/850 — Kosovo visa liberalisation (effective 1 January 2024)
EU Regulation 2025/11 — Vanuatu transferred to Annex I (effective 14 January 2025)

validation_notes: ADDED Kosovo (XKX) to visa_free: visa-free from 1 January 2024 per EU Regulation 2023/850 with biometric passport — was absent from prior record.

ADDED Albania (ALB), Bosnia and Herzegovina (BIH), Georgia (GEO), Moldova (MDA), Montenegro (MNE), North Macedonia (MKD), Serbia (SRB), Ukraine (UKR) to visa_free: all are in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 and were entirely absent from the prior record.
ADDED Nauru (NRU) to visa_free: listed in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806, was absent from prior record.
ADDED Taiwan (TWN), Hong Kong SAR, Macao SAR to visa_free: all listed in Annex II (under 'Other Entities' and 'Special Administrative Regions' sections), were absent from prior record.

CONFIRMED Vanuatu correctly absent from visa_free: Regulation (EU) 2025/11 permanently moved Vanuatu to Annex I (visa required), effective 14 January 2025. Prior record's notes referenced a temporary suspension; updated to permanent removal.

CORRECTED diplomatic/service passport conditional_access: prior record was significantly incomplete (missing Algeria, Cambodia, Congo, Cote d'Ivoire, Cuba, Egypt, Gabon, Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, India, Iran, Jordan, Mongolia, Oman, Philippines, Qatar) and had wrong passport type data for several countries (Bolivia, Cambodia, China, Ecuador, Indonesia were missing 'special' type; Kazakhstan, Jamaica had wrong type data). Updated to match EUR-Lex 2023XC0927(01) BNL table.

REMOVED Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus from conditional_access diplomatic passport entries: these countries appear in the EU visa facilitation agreement context but were listed with EU-Armenia/Azerbaijan/Belarus framework references — the EUR-Lex 2023XC0927 table fetched does not show them under BNL column; removed to avoid incorrect attribution. Flagged as gap.
CORRECTED China entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Bolivia entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic, service' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Indonesia entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic, service' to 'diplomatic, service, special' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Kazakhstan entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
CORRECTED Pakistan entry in conditional_access: confirmed as 'diplomatic, service' (prior had 'diplomatic only').
CORRECTED Senegal entry in conditional_access: changed from 'diplomatic only' to 'diplomatic, service' per EUR-Lex derogations table.
ADDED India to conditional_access with diplomatic, service passports per EUR-Lex derogations table — was absent from prior record.
UPDATED ETIAS note: clarified that ETIAS is expected operational Q4 2026 with mandatory enforcement from April 2027.
gaps: Belgium's official IBZ website does not publish a standalone enumerated list of visa-free and visa-required nationalities; the authoritative reference is EU Regulation 2018/1806 on EUR-Lex.

The Armenia, Azerbaijan, and Belarus diplomatic passport exemptions listed in the prior record via EU visa facilitation agreements (OJ references) could not be confirmed in the EUR-Lex 2023XC0927(01) BNL/Belgium column table during this review — they have been removed pending re-verification against the full EU derogations table.
Regulation 2025/2441 (entered into force December 2025) updated the visa suspension mechanism rules but does not change the Annex II country list — no country-list impact confirmed.

The full diplomatic/service passport derogations table from EUR-Lex 52023XC0927(01) is a notification as of July 2023; a 2024 or 2025 updated version may exist but was not accessible for direct comparison.
Processing times for Researcher and ICT permits are not stated on the official IBZ pages consulted.
Dual citizenship rules for Belgium naturalization not confirmed from official source during this research session.
ETIAS launch date and mandatory enforcement date (expected Q4 2026 / April 2027) subject to change; not yet operational as of June 2026.
Exact bilateral agreement dates for non-EU-framework diplomatic passport waivers are not published on the official IBZ or diplomatie.belgium.be pages.
No OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder exemption found in any official Belgian or EU source.
